result.root_cause: The site styles each of the six OTP entry boxes as a fixed-width `<input type="number">` (ids `0-otp`..`5-otp`, class `otp-field-input`, `width:25px !important`) and tries to hide the native number spin box so a single digit fits. But it only hides the spinner in two ways that both fail in Firefox: (1) via the WebKit/Blink-only pseudo-elements `::-webkit-inner-spin-button` / `::-webkit-outer-spin-button { -webkit-appearance:none }`, which Firefox does not implement, and (2) via `.otp-widget input[type=number] { -moz-appearance:none }` / `appearance:none` on the input. The key engine difference: in Firefox `appearance:none` (and `-moz-appearance:none`) does NOT suppress the number-input spin box — only `appearance:textfield` (`-moz-appearance:textfield`) removes it. So in Firefox the native up/down spinner remains and, inside the fixed 25px box, consumes the entire content area, leaving effectively zero width for the digit. Result: each field renders as an up/down spinner ("drop-down" look) and the typed digit is not visible. Chrome removes the spinner via the `::-webkit-inner-spin-button` reset, leaving usable content width so the digit shows. This is a site CSS bug relying on a non-standard/engine-specific spinner-removal technique rather than the standardized `appearance:textfield`. The digit is still stored in the input value; it is purely a rendering/visibility problem caused by the spinner occupying the box.
result.firefox_findings: On the real site (Firefox 153, headless), after entering an email and clicking "Get OTP" (POST to https://www.jobs.abbott/widgets, ddoKey getToken, "Email sent successfully"), six `<input type="number">` OTP fields render (id `0-otp`..`5-otp`, class `otp-field-input`, autocomplete one-time-code). Computed on `0-otp`: type=number, appearance=none, -moz-appearance=none, offsetWidth=25, clientWidth=0, scrollWidth=0 — the native number spin box fills the whole 25px content box so no digit is visible; the box looks like an up/down spinner/drop-down. In the reduced test case (2053077-default.html) the `broken` input with `appearance:none` gave clientWidth=1/offsetWidth=25 and the screenshot showed only spinner arrows with no digit, whereas the `fixed` control input with `appearance:textfield` gave clientWidth=19 and correctly displayed the digit "5" — proving the spinner is only removed in Firefox by `appearance:textfield`, not `appearance:none`. Console noise on the live page (SecurityError, transient jQuery-not-defined, getSubmenu null) is unrelated.
result.chrome_findings: Chrome (HeadlessChrome 151, --no-sandbox) with identical steps works: the same getToken POST returns "Email sent successfully" and the same six `<input type="number">` OTP boxes render, but they appear as normal single-character boxes with a placeholder and no spinner arrows. Computed on `0-otp`: type=number, appearance=none, offsetWidth=25, clientWidth=23, scrollWidth=23 — the `::-webkit-inner-spin-button` reset removes the spinner in Blink, leaving usable width for the digit. In the reduced test case, both the `broken` (appearance:none) and `fixed` (appearance:textfield) inputs reported clientWidth=23 and displayed the digit, confirming Chrome removes the spinner regardless via the WebKit pseudo-element reset.
result.difference: Same HTML and CSS in both browsers; the divergence is purely in how each engine renders `<input type="number">` with `appearance:none` inside a fixed 25px width. Chrome removes the spin box (via `::-webkit-inner-spin-button{-webkit-appearance:none}`) → clientWidth≈23, digit visible. Firefox does not implement that pseudo-element and treats `appearance:none`/`-moz-appearance:none` as NOT removing the number spin box (only `appearance:textfield` does), so the spinner stays and fills the box → clientWidth 0/1, digit hidden, field looks like a drop-down. The observable metric is `0-otp`/`#broken` clientWidth: ~0-1 in Firefox vs ~23 in Chrome, with identical offsetWidth=25. The control input using `appearance:textfield` yields clientWidth 19 in Firefox, demonstrating the exact standard technique the site failed to use.
result.confidence: high — the reduced test case isolates the single CSS declaration, and the appearance:none vs appearance:textfield control input directly demonstrates the engine-specific spinner-removal behavior driving the symptom.
result.steps:
1. Read the reproduction script /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-qe99ny70/2053077-default.repro.mjs, which drives the real site in both browsers and measures `0-otp` (clientWidth 0 in Firefox vs 23 in Chrome, offsetWidth 25 in both).
2. From the findings, identified the suspect: the OTP boxes are `<input type="number">` at fixed width:25px whose spinner is removed only via `::-webkit-inner-spin-button` (Blink/WebKit only) and `-moz-appearance:none`/`appearance:none` (which does not remove the Firefox number spinner).
3. Wrote a minimal reduced test case to /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-qe99ny70/2053077-default.html with two fixed 25px `<input type="number">`: `#broken` styled like the site (appearance:none + webkit spin-button reset) and `#fixed` control using appearance:textfield; plus a `window.__probe()` returning clientWidth/offsetWidth of each and the user agent.
4. Loaded the file via file:// URL in Firefox (firefox-devtools navigate_page) and Chrome (chrome-devtools navigate_page).
5. Ran `window.__probe()` in both: Firefox → broken clientWidth=1, fixed clientWidth=19; Chrome → broken clientWidth=23, fixed clientWidth=23 (offsetWidth=25 everywhere). This reproduces the site's Firefox/Chrome divergence.
6. Took a Firefox screenshot showing `#broken` as bare up/down spinner arrows (no digit) and `#fixed` showing the digit "5" — matching the reported "OTP fields appear as drop-downs / numbers not displayed" symptom, and confirming appearance:textfield is the technique that would work in Firefox.
7. Concluded the root cause is the site's reliance on non-standard/engine-specific number-spinner removal (webkit pseudo-element + appearance:none) instead of the standard appearance:textfield, and submitted this diagnosis.

result.root_cause:
The site displays the "Member Login" pop-up purely with CSS driven by the URL fragment: .modal { opacity:0; visibility:hidden } and .modal:target { opacity:1; visibility:visible }. No JavaScript/class toggles visibility. The page is opened already at #login, and <div class="modal" id="login"> is present in the server-rendered HTML, so at parse time it is the document's fragment target. However, the whole <body id="app"> is a Vue 2.5.17 app: synchronous scripts at the end of <body> (js/login.js new Vue({el:'#main-area'}), js/page_init.js, etc.) mount Vue, which compiles the in-DOM template and REPLACES the parsed #login node with a brand-new element that has the same id="login". This replacement happens AFTER the browser's parse-time fragment/:target resolution but BEFORE the load event.

The browser divergence is in when :target is (re)resolved for the dynamically recreated element. Instrumenting the real site in Chrome showed: at DOMContentLoaded #login is present but #login.matches(':target') === false; then between DOMContentLoaded and the load/readyState==='complete' phase Chrome re-resolves the fragment and :target flips to true, so .modal:target applies and the modal becomes visible. Firefox resolves the fragment target only once (parse time). Once the original target node is removed by Vue, the target is lost and Firefox does NOT re-resolve :target to the new same-id element at load, so .modal:target never matches and the modal stays opacity:0/visibility:hidden. Because the page already loads at #login, clicking the "Member Login" trigger (an <a href="#login">) does not change the hash and therefore never fires a hashchange to re-evaluate :target, so the pop-up stays broken on both load and click. Only a genuine hash change (location.hash='' then location.hash='login') forces Firefox to re-resolve the target, which makes the modal appear — confirming the mechanism. The Firefox-only [Vue warn]: Error compiling template and the shared ReferenceError: url is not defined in the console are unrelated: the #login element and its content are identical in both browsers; the only difference is the :target match state.
result.firefox_findings: On a clean load of https://www.centuryasia.com.tw/login.html?obj=l&obj1=i&ver=I+DfKKUJFcA=#login the Member Login modal does not appear (only the header/hero renders). DOM probe of #login: element present with the full login form content, but getComputedStyle reports visibility:hidden and opacity:0, and #login.matches(':target') === false even though location.hash === "#login" and the element's id === "login". Enumerating stylesheet rules confirmed the modal is controlled solely by .modal { opacity:0; visibility:hidden } and .modal:target { opacity:1; visibility:visible } with no JS/class toggle. Firing a fresh hashchange (location.hash='' then location.hash='login') flips #login.matches(':target') to true and the modal renders (visibility:visible, opacity:1), proving the symptom is the :target mismatch. In my reduced test case, on a clean load at #login, Firefox reported matches(:target)=false, visibility:hidden, opacity:0, MODAL VISIBLE=false (no modal box shown). Console also shows a Firefox-only [Vue warn]: Error compiling template plus a shared ReferenceError: url is not defined, but neither is causal — the modal element and content are identical to Chrome.
result.chrome_findings: On the same clean load, Chrome displays the Member Login pop-up. DOM probe of #login: same element/content as Firefox, but getComputedStyle reports visibility:visible and opacity:1 and #login.matches(':target') === true with location.hash === "#login", so .modal:target applies. Instrumenting the real site with an initScript that logs #login presence/:target/visibility across lifecycle events revealed the precise timing: at initScript (readyState=loading) #login absent; at DOMContentLoaded (readyState=interactive) #login present but isTarget=false, visibility=hidden; at readyState=complete/load isTarget flips to true; ~300ms later visibility=visible. A MutationObserver confirmed the currently-visible #login is NOT the originally-parsed node (sameAsObserved=false), i.e. it was recreated. So Chrome performs an additional fragment/:target resolution at load completion that catches the recreated element. My reduced test case matched: on a clean load at #login, Chrome reported matches(:target)=true, visibility:visible, opacity:1, MODAL VISIBLE=true (modal box shown).
result.difference: Both browsers receive identical HTML/CSS/JS and both end up with an #login element (recreated by Vue) whose id equals the URL fragment. The only observable divergence is the CSS :target match state on that recreated element: Chrome re-resolves the document's fragment target at the load/complete phase (observed #login.matches(':target') flipping false→true between DOMContentLoaded and load), so .modal:target applies and the modal is visible; Firefox resolves the fragment target only once at parse time and never re-resolves it to the dynamically-recreated same-id node, so .modal:target never matches and the modal stays opacity:0/visibility:hidden. Timing is decisive: controlled reductions showed that if the same-id element is replaced DURING parse (before target resolution) both browsers match :target, and if replaced AFTER load both fail to match; the bug reproduces only in the in-between window (replacement at DOMContentLoaded), which is exactly when the real site's Vue app recreates the node.
result.confidence: high — the reduced test case reproduces the exact Firefox-hidden / Chrome-visible split on clean loads, and instrumented real-site lifecycle logs show Chrome's :target re-resolution (false→true at load) that Firefox lacks.
result.steps:
1. Read the reproduction script at /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-m_41nvjf/2040305-default.repro.mjs to confirm the reported flow: load https://www.centuryasia.com.tw/login.html?obj=l&obj1=i&ver=I+DfKKUJFcA=#login and check whether #login is visible via computed opacity/visibility and matches(':target').
2. Fetched the raw page HTML with curl of https://www.centuryasia.com.tw/login.html?obj=l&obj1=i&ver=I+DfKKUJFcA= and confirmed <div class="modal" id="login"> is statically present (line 214) and that <body id="app"> is a Vue root, with synchronous end-of-body scripts js/page_init.js and js/login.js.
3. Fetched js/page_init.js and js/login.js (curl of https://www.centuryasia.com.tw/js/login.js?0fKKApRlrx8= etc.) and found new Vue({el:'#main-area'}) (login.js) plus #header-area/#footer-area Vue instances — confirming Vue compiles the in-DOM template and recreates #login.
4. Wrote a first reduced HTML (a purely dynamically-created #login appended after load) to /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-m_41nvjf/2040305-default.html; loaded it at #login in both browsers via the DevTools navigate/evaluate tools — both browsers gave :target=false (did NOT reproduce). Ruled out "purely dynamic element".
5. Revised the reduction to replace a static #login after load — both browsers :target=false; then replace during parse (synchronous inline script) — both browsers :target=true. Neither reproduced. Concluded timing of the swap is the deciding factor.
6. Instrumented the REAL site in Chrome: navigated (after about:blank) with an initScript that logs #login presence, matches(':target'), and visibility at initScript/readystatechange/DOMContentLoaded/load, plus a MutationObserver for #login add/remove. Observed: #login absent at parse start; present but :target=false at DOMContentLoaded; :target flips to true at readyState=complete/load; the visible node is not the originally-parsed node. This pinned the swap window to "after parse-time resolution, before load", with Chrome re-resolving :target at load.
7. Rewrote the reduced test case (/tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-m_41nvjf/2040305-default.html) to match: a static <div class="modal" id="login"> plus CSS .modal{opacity:0;visibility:hidden} / .modal:target{opacity:1;visibility:visible}, and a DOMContentLoaded handler that removes the original #login and inserts a new element with the same id="login"; a load handler then reports matches(':target') and computed visibility.
8. Loaded the reduced case cleanly (navigated each browser to about:blank first, then to file:///tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-m_41nvjf/2040305-default.html#login) in both Firefox and Chrome and probed with evaluate_script after the load handler ran: Firefox matches(:target)=false, visibility:hidden, opacity:0, MODAL VISIBLE=false; Chrome matches(:target)=true, visibility:visible, opacity:1, MODAL VISIBLE=true. Screenshots confirmed the modal box renders in Chrome and is absent in Firefox — the difference is reproduced.
9. Concluded root cause: CSS :target-driven modal + Vue recreating the #login fragment-target node after parse-time resolution; Chrome re-resolves :target at load while Firefox does not, and since the page loads already at #login no hashchange ever re-fires :target (so clicking the <a href="#login"> trigger cannot fix it).

result.root_cause: The game is embedded in an iframe: <iframe src="/game/index.html" class="aspect-video max-h-full max-w-full">. This iframe is a replaced element that (a) has an intrinsic aspect ratio from aspect-ratio:16/9, (b) has NO intrinsic/natural dimensions (an iframe has no natural size, and this one has no width/height attributes), and (c) has both CSS width and height computed to auto (only max-width:100% / max-height:100% are set). This is precisely the case CSS2.1 section 10.3.2 (Inline, replaced elements) declares UNDEFINED: when both width and height are auto and the element has an intrinsic ratio but no intrinsic dimensions, the used width is undefined, though the spec suggests computing it as for block-level non-replaced elements (fill the containing block width). Chrome follows that suggestion and stretches the iframe to fill the containing-block width (constrained by the ratio and max-*), so the game renders full-size. Firefox instead falls back to the replaced-element default object size (300x150), applies the ratio to get 300x168.75, and keeps the iframe ~300px wide regardless of container size, squeezing the game into a tiny box where internal UI overlaps and is unscaled. This is not caused by console errors, failed network requests, UA sniffing, or an unsupported API: the DOM and computed CSS are identical in both browsers; only the used/rendered iframe size differs.
result.firefox_findings: On https://drmeth.com/ the whole game is collapsed into a tiny ~300x169px box centered in an otherwise empty gray stage; internal elements overlap and are unscaled, matching the report screenshot. No relevant console errors and no failed network requests. DOM probing of iframe.aspect-video: getComputedStyle reports width=300px, height=168.75px, aspect-ratio 16/9, max-width=100%, max-height=100%, flex 0 1 auto, min-width/min-height=auto, no width/height attribute. Its parent flex container (div.flex.flex-1.items-center.justify-center.overflow-hidden) is 1366px wide, yet the iframe stays at 300px. In my reduced test case, Firefox rendered the iframe at 300px in BOTH a flex container and a plain block container, and even with only max-height:100% set, so the behavior is independent of flexbox.
result.chrome_findings: On the same page and steps, Chrome renders the game full-size, filling the content area; the layout looks correct. Same DOM and same computed CSS on the iframe (aspect-ratio 16/9, max-width=100%, max-height=100%, flex 0 1 auto, no explicit width/height), but getComputedStyle reports the iframe width as 1905px (equal to the flex container) and height ~1071px. In my reduced test case Chrome sized the iframe to fill the 800px-wide stage (800px, or 1685px when height-limited) in both flex and block containers and with max-height only. Chrome resolves the auto width to the stretch-fit containing-block width, then derives height from the aspect ratio.
result.difference: Identical markup and identical computed CSS produce different USED sizes for the game iframe. Firefox sizes it to the replaced-element default object size (300x150 -> 300x168.75 after the 16/9 ratio) and ignores available container width; Chrome stretches it to fill the containing block (constrained by ratio and max-*). The divergence disappears in control cases where CSS is well-defined: (1) with NO aspect-ratio both browsers use 300x150; (2) with explicit width/height attributes (intrinsic dimensions present) both use 160x90; (3) an img with an intrinsic SVG size does not diverge. The divergence is isolated to exactly intrinsic-ratio + no-intrinsic-dimensions + width/height auto, which the CSS spec leaves undefined.
result.confidence: high — a minimal reduced test case reproduces the exact Firefox-300px vs Chrome-fills split, and adding/removing single properties (aspect-ratio, intrinsic dimensions) makes the two browsers converge, pinpointing the spec-undefined sizing case as the sole cause.
result.steps:
1. Read the reproduction script at /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-m3i3dg0y/2052489-default.repro.mjs to understand the reported iframe-sizing divergence.
2. Navigated both Firefox and Chrome (via their DevTools MCP tools) to https://drmeth.com/.
3. Ran an evaluate_script probe in each browser walking the ancestor chain of iframe.aspect-video, capturing getComputedStyle (width, height, aspect-ratio, max-width/height, flex, min-*) and getBoundingClientRect. Found: Firefox iframe=300x169 inside a 1366px flex container; Chrome iframe=1905x1072 inside the same-width container. Confirmed identical computed CSS; only used size differs. No relevant console errors / failed requests.
4. Wrote a minimal reduced test case to /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-m3i3dg0y/2052489-default.html: a column-flex body containing a row-flex centered stage holding an iframe.replaced styled only with aspect-ratio:16/9; max-width:100%; max-height:100% (no width/height), plus a comparison img with an intrinsic 16x9 SVG, and inline JS that prints each element measured width.
5. Loaded the test case in both browsers (file://) and probed measured sizes: Firefox iframe=300px (tiny), Chrome iframe=1685px (fills, height-limited); the img was 16x9 in BOTH (no divergence), showing it is specific to a replaced element with no intrinsic size.
6. Ran a further evaluate_script harness in each browser dynamically creating stages to isolate factors: iframe in a block container (FF=300, Chrome=800), iframe in a flex container (FF=300, Chrome=800), a non-replaced div (both 0), and iframe with max-height only (FF=300, Chrome=800), proving the divergence is NOT flex-specific.
7. Ran a final control probe: iframe WITHOUT aspect-ratio (both browsers=300x150) and iframe WITH explicit width/height attributes + aspect-ratio (both browsers=160x90), proving the browsers agree except in the intrinsic-ratio-but-no-intrinsic-dimensions case.
8. Concluded the root cause is the CSS2.1 section 10.3.2 spec-undefined sizing of a replaced element with an intrinsic ratio but no intrinsic dimensions: Chrome stretch-fits to the containing block, Firefox falls back to the 300px default object size.
